sentences.text: Yes.
Well, the ghetto was not yet officially established.
But we were told to-- to wear our bands, arm bands with the-- with the Star of David.
I don't.
I am sure, yeah.
In Poland, always there was this.
When you lived in a-- in a house, you had to be registered there, living there.
Yeah.
But I don't remember these-- these details.
Yes.
So I mentioned to you that there was no money.
And food became a problem very, very soon.
There was no money to-- to attend classes for me.
I was 12.
Going out was not very safe.
In the beginning during the first few months, there were some little groups of Polish hooligans.
Very often they were actually organized or partially organized by the Germans, who roamed the streets and-- and pick up Jews.
I remember once I left home to-- to go somewhere.
And we lived fairly close to Ogrod Krasinski, which is a-- a little garden, a little park, of [NON-ENGLISH].
Krasinksi was a famous Polish poet.
And this was not part of the ghetto, but-- of the Jewish quarter.
But it was just adjacent to the Jewish quarter.
So I remember to cut through this-- through this-- I decided to cut through this park.
And I noticed there, a, a group, a large group of people watching.
And I had the presence of mind to pull off my armband.
And I approached it.
And I saw a person bloodied, you know, being-- being beaten.
And I saw a German making a picture of it.
So what they did was they would then say, well, this is what's happening.
The poles are beating up the Jews.
So we have to-- to really protect the Jews in the ghetto.
We have to create a ghetto.
This is all types of small tactics that they used this way.
So going out was not very safe.
The ghetto was swelling.
The ghetto population was swelling.
It was the deportation from little towns and villages around ghetto.
The Jews were being deported from there.
And they all flocked into-- into the Polish, into the Warsaw-- Warsaw ghetto.
These people were the first victim of typhus and-- and hunger.
They may have been quite well-to-do in their towns.
But everything was taken from them.
Many of them were killed on their way.
And when they came, they became beggars.
And they became homeless.
Because the Judenrat, the Jewish Judenrat, whatever it's called in-- in English--
Committee, organization.
--could not, even though they, I believe, they tried to-- to place these people somewhere.
But it was very hard.
In addition, the ghetto was starting already.
And Jews who lived all over Poland, all over Warsaw were also moving into the ghetto, some Poles moving out.
So as I said, there were homeless, homeless people.
And as the time went on, the 1940, the middle of 1940, the size of these people was absolutely terrible.
There were children.
There were starving children, little skeletons with swollen bellies begging in the street.
You could see entire families.
And you could see a family that started as a large family, maybe six.
And then the children were dying off.
Or, occasionally, you could see just a child or a couple of children left and the bodies, the corpses, of their parents lying in the streets.
There were many corpses lying in the street sometimes covered by a newspaper.
Yeah.
Well, the Judenrat was responsible.
There was a collection.
Every early morning, there was a collection.
They would just throw these bodies on a wagon.
Yes.
I am.
And they would bury them in a common grave.
But what was terribly disturbing to me was the-- the wailing of the-- of the children.
They-- they even ignored the curfew.
We could hear this wailing, this lament, constantly in our room up on the fourth floor.
This was driving me crazy.
I-- I was trying to read.
This was the only thing I could do.
There were some books in the house.
But this was very, very hard.
Yes.
Yes.
There was a contact.
And there was one--
Through the mail.
There was but very few somehow.
You know, there were some limitations.
And there was one.
I-- I remember one food package that came.
And this was great rejoicing.
I remember the Russian sugar.
It was a hard ball, the head of-- of sugar, very, very hard.
Yeah.
But this was-- there was very little.
I believe they were limited in the amount they could send us.
And also there was some limitation.
I don't remember how many letters we got.
But we knew that my father is in Bialystok.
He was a editor of a paper called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a Yiddish newspaper.
I knew that my brother goes to school.
And he is going to matriculate there.
Well, of course, you know, we always lived with a hope that we will join them.
But in the meantime, we had to live somehow.
For a while, well, I talked about-- about the children and about-- about the beggars.
All that was going on in front of cafes, restaurants, and nightclubs.
There was an entire new class of nouveau riche in the ghetto.
They were--
Jewish profiteers who took advantage of the shortages, food shortages, who made their dirty business with Germans, with Poles, Polish scum of the Polish society.
And, oh, they-- they had champagne and dances and balls and food.
And I was so young.
I don't know what I made out of it.
It's very hard, you know, for me to-- to go back and to think what I as a 13-- maybe-- year-old child looked at.
But I-- I know that I was terribly disturbed by the-- by the children by the sight and the sound of these children.
And I remember once I-- I was asked to babysit a little girl.
Her mother had to go out.
This was a friend of my mother, whoever.
And I was there along with the child.
And a little skeleton beggar in rags came.
They were all over.
And there was a piece of bread in the basket.
And without any thought or hesitation, I just gave this child this bread.
It turned out when this lady came back, the mother of the child, that this was the only food she had for the entire day.
And I felt terrible.
This is how it was.
Well we had to eat something.
And my mother, again, performed, you know, little miracles trying to feed me.
We used to go for a while to the Jewish writer's union.
It was at that time on street that was called Karmelitska.
This Karmelitska was a very narrow street and very, very crowded always.
Of course, it was crowded with people going after their business.
It was crowded with beggars.
And at that time, they started snatching whatever you had.
If there was a little package in your hands, they would snatch it looking for food.
If it was not food, they would drop it.
It was food, they would devour it immediately.
The-- but in addition the Germans liked to drive through this Karmelitska street shooting into the crowd.
This was a perfect street, because there was just no-- there was such a dense crowd.
So we were going there to-- to this writer's union, because they had a soup kitchen.
And it was good food.
But after a few months, my mother decided that it's too dangerous to cross the street.
So we stopped going there also.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then my mother found a family that would allow me to come once a week for dinner.
This was a fairly wealthy family.
I hated it.
I hated it, because they were not nice people to start with.
The food was very good.
Occasionally, I would try to-- let's say I got a-- a piece of meat.
And I tried to save some for my mother and put it in paper napkins.
And they would all look at me.
And I-- I just refused to go there.
And then there was another family that she found.
She never stopped.
And this was a nicer place.
And they had a lot of books.
No.
No, but I remember the books that I read there.
I read, of course, in Polish translation, the entire French classical literature.
This was my-- this was something that I--
--liked to do.
And-- and it was a perfect escape.
I remember reading Balzac and being completely involved, you know, in this other world.
Yes.
Yes.
[SIGHS] What else about the Warsaw ghetto?
Well, of course, you know, the-- the ghetto became closed.
The walls were going on all the time.
The walls were very tall.
There was a glass-- broken glass and barbed wires around on above the wall.
Entry and exit from the ghetto was-- was--
There were, yeah.
There were several entries and exits.
And I talk about this because I went to one.
Yeah.
These exits were guarded by Germans, Polish police, and Jewish police.
Of course, the Jewish police was already organized, the Judenrat.
And Czerniakow, a very famous name, was the head of the Judenrat.
And you know that he committed suicide when-- when the action started, when-- when they wanted him to supply the list of people to go first.
And he probably tried to his best before that, but--
Oh, they just did everything that the Germans told them to do.
This is the way I saw it.
They were collecting money, organizing the police, all types of things.
And then later on when Czerniakow killed himself, but he was the-- the only one.
And all the others went on providing lists, providing all types of information, trying-- I don't know-- to save their lives or to save the lives?
Maybe they believed, maybe they did.
I mean, the ghetto was full of rumors.
[INAUDIBLE] it was throughout the war, all types of false rumors.
If we do this, they'll stop the action.
If we give them this money or if we give them 10,000 people for a transport, they will not touch the others.
The Germans would create this lie.
And people wanted to believe it I guess.
So some of these things are absolutely ridiculous.
Well, not yet.
I-- when I was-- I-- I left the ghetto in 1941.
And until then, I did not hear.
But people were being murdered over there.
And they were being sent off.
But the talk was about labor camps.
OK.
So as the time went on, our hopes for this visa to join my father was dwindling.
And then it died, of course, completely when the Germans attacked Russia on the 22nd of-- of June, 1941.
At that point, we lived alone already in this room.
Because the other woman with her child went to live with her family.
She wanted to be close to-- to her family.
She was from Warsaw.
We lived alone.
And we started to be blackmailed by Polish police.
There was this policeman who came and, eh, who told us that he knows everything, of course, about our correspondence and-- and connections with Russia and communism and what not.
And he will give us up if we don't pay.
Well, we didn't have anything.
We didn't have anything.
So he came few more times.
And somehow he-- he probably came to the conclusion that we really don't have anything.
And he was maybe decent enough, you know, to leave us alone and not to give us up, not to do anything to us.
Because he stopped coming.
But this was-- finally that, you know, convinced my mother.
My mother decided that we have no future here.
It's going-- we are going to die if we stay here.
Where to go?
We didn't have any money.
We couldn't stay on the Aryan side.
We didn't know people.
We couldn't stay in the ghetto.
We'll go back to Czestochowa.
We have to go back to [?
Czestochowa ?].
And we knew that there is a ghetto in Czestochowa, too.
But at least there there was some family.
And it was a familiar place.
So I left first.
And it was all arranged by my mother.
So I really don't know.
And I regret that I did not ask my mother when she was still alive all these details that I would like to know now.
But somehow, you know, I didn't think about it.
Anyhow, what she arranged was that I was supposed to be in the ghetto gate.
This was a gate on corner of [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
These were the streets.
There was a barrier there and the Germans gendarmes and the Jewish policeman, and the Polish policeman.
And I was to be there at 10:00 or around 10:00.
And I was to have a book.
And in the book, there was money.
And I was to approach the Jewish policeman and open the book.
And then they would let me through.
And I also knew that on the outside there is a young man, a Pole, who waits for me.
She must have had some contacts.
And this is what happened.
My mother was standing far away just watching.
I mean, these places, the-- the ghetto gates were always surrounded by crowds of people.

All types of things were happening there, some exchanges things for bread, you know, et cetera, a lot of killing, like little-- little, Jewish smugglers who-- the beggars who would go out and smuggle some food.
But when caught, they would be beaten or killed right there.
Anyhow, my mother was standing there and watching me.
I can't imagine what she felt at that point.
And I don't remember what I felt.
I just don't remember.
I-- I went.
I had--
Any parting word?
I don't remember.
Because I knew that she will follow me, you know, if she can.
And my book was Homer's I loved this book.
And I came.
I-- as I said, I opened the book.
The Jewish policeman took the money.
And I was out.
And the German generals were in the sentry box.
So it was obviously, you know, they were making business together.
They were dividing.
Yeah.
And this young man was waiting for me.
This was terribly important, because the ghetto gates and the stations, train stations, were terribly dangerous.
They were swarming with spies, with informers, with blackmailers, with just Germans, police, all types of people.
And blackmail of Jews was very, unfortunately, common.
So, he--
No.
No.
He took me to the station.
He took me in a [? tram ?].
We were traveling [INAUDIBLE] It was all very-- I mean, for two years I was behind the walls.
And I-- I-- I did feel strange in this seemingly normal street even though, you know, in the Polish streets things were going on, too, constant, round ups of young people and-- but it was different.
So he took me to the station.
And he bought me a ticket.
And this was also terribly important.
Because you needed papers to buy a train ticket.
And then he asked me if I would be all right.
And I said, yes, thank you very much.
And he left.
So I was left alone.
I was dressed very stupidly.
And this was a mistake that many Jews made.
It was probably October.
But it was a very warm October. 1941?
And I was in my winter coat, because my mother decided that I shouldn't have lots of stuff, just a very small backpack.
And the winter coat-- coat was heavy, so I should wear it.
But this attracted attention, of course, you know, a winter coat.
And I looked-- I had a good look, so-called good look.
I still had my braids.
I was blonde at that time.
And I don't have any specific Semitic features.
No.
I didn't have false identification.
So I didn't carry any.
So maybe 15 minutes-- I still have a couple-- had a couple of hours to wait for-- for the train to come.
And maybe 15 minutes after he left, I was surrounded by a-- by a group of Polish boys.
They were 10, 12 years old.
And they started to tell me, oh, you are-- you are a kike, you know, you're a Jew.
Give us something.
If not, we are going to take you to-- somehow, I don't know, maybe because they were a little bit younger than I was, I collected myself.
And I started screaming at them.
I told them, you such and such.
You get away from me.
Because I'm going to take you to the police.
And I did get rid of them.
It was-- you know, it could have happened the other way around.
But maybe because the way I looked and I sounded-- my Polish, of course, was impeccable.
And maybe they-- because of that, they left me.
Well, finally, the train arrived.
And I boarded the train.
At the beginning, there were not many people inside.
And I was standing at the window and watching the fields that I didn't see for a long time.
But then people-- at the small stations, people started to come in, peasants.
Many women that looked very big.
And it was obvious that they were smuggling food to the city under their clothes.
Some of them had baskets.
And after another while, I-- I was sort of-- I was so different from this crowd with my coat and standing at the window and not talking with anybody.
And they started to talk to me.
At the beginning, sort of a friendly way, sort of laughing, you know, and where do you go?
Where are you from?
Such a nice [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means mademoiselle.
They would-- they would not say you to me.
They would say [NON-ENGLISH].
But I-- I blocked.
I just stopped talking.
And then they looked at me again.
And they told me, oh, you must be a little Jewish girl who is escaping from the ghetto.
And then other people started to watch.
And I saw there was somebody sitting there and reading and started to look.
And I felt all the eyes on me.
And then I-- I felt that they were getting less friendly.
And the word Jew was already pronounced.
And I was terrified.
